reasoning: The most recent value of the ICE BofA AA US Corporate Index Option-Adjusted Spread is 0.56 (as of May 9), having fallen from a peak of 0.62 in late April. The current trend suggests short-term mean-reversion, pushing the immediate probability of the spread increasing above 0.56 slightly below 50%. The FRED trend analysis provides an estimate of P(>0.56) = 0.44 for the near term, which serves as a highly reliable anchor. Over longer horizons, uncertainty increases. Historically spreads are heavily right-skewed, meaning that while they frequently sit at baseline levels, macroeconomic shocks (like trade warnings or recession fears) can cause substantial spikes. Consequently, as the forecast horizon pushes out to 3-6 months, the uncertainty domain strictly increases and the forecast approaches the uninformative prior of 0.5, effectively balancing the downward short-term momentum against the longer-term upside risk of a shock.
